coredata.dc:title: An efficient fluorescent probe for fluazinam using N, S co-doped carbon dots from l-cysteine
coredata.dc:creator: Zou, Shiying
Hou, Changjun
Fa, Huanbao
Zhang, Liang
Ma, Yi
Dong, Liang
Li, Dan
Huo, Danqun
Yang, Mei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur (NSCDs) was prepared from l-cysteine with a hydrothermal method and applied to develop a fluorescent sensor to determine fluazinam. Characterizations of the NSCDs suggest that it provides abundant affinitive sites for recognizing fluazinam through molecular interactions including electrostatic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions. Strong inner filter effect (IFE) leads fluazinam to quench the fluorescence of the NSCDs with high efficiency. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescent probe shows very good sensitivity and selectivity in a linear range from 0.05μM to 4μM, and the detection limit is calculated down to 10nM. The proposed fluazinam sensor is demonstrated to be feasible for application in detecting this fungicide in soil and apple samples with satisfactory accuracy. Besides, NSCDs could also be used to fabricate visual paper-based testing stripes under a portable UV lamp with a detection limit as lower as 50μM. The fluorescent material presented here provides a functional platform for simple yet facile in-field detection of fluazinam in related samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluazinam
Fluorescent probe
N, S Co-doped carbon dots
Hydrothermal reaction
Fungicide

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of fluazinam in soil and apple samples
Analytical performance of the NSCDs probe for fluazinam detection
Selectivity of the NSCDs probe for fluazinam detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images (A), HRTEM image (B), size distribution (C) and XRD (D) of the as-obtained NSCDs.
FT-IR spectrum of the as-obtained NSCDs.
XPS spectra of NSCDs. (A) full scan XPS spectrum. (B-D) High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s (B), N1s (C) and (D) S 2p.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of the as-prepared NSCDs. Inset shows the photographs of NSCDs aqueous solution under UV light of 312nm (1) and visible light(2). (B) Emission spectra of the NSCDs under different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300nm to 400nm. Inset: The normalized FL emission spectra of the NSCDs. (C) (I) UV–vis absorption spectrum of fluazinam in 0.05M Na2CO3-0·1MNaHCO3 (pH=8.8) buffer solution and fluorescence excitation (II) and emission (III)spectra of NSCDs.

Effect of pH (A), buffer medium (1:0.1M glycine-0.1M NaCl-0.1M NaOH; 2: 0.1M KH2PO4-0.05M H3BO3; 3: 0.1M NaOH-0.2M H3BO3-KCl; 4: 0.05M Na2CO3-0.1M NaHCO3; 5:0.1M NH4Cl-0.1M NH3·H2O) (B), volume of buffer medium 0.05M Na2CO3-0.1M NaHCO3 (C), Incubation temperature (D) and Incubation time (E).

(A) Emission spectra of NSCDs in the presence of different concentration of fluazinam (from 0 to 6μM) in aqueous solution. (B) F0/F of NSCDs (λex=350nm, λem=440nm) mixed with different concentration of fluazinam versus the concentration of fluazinam in Na2CO3-NaHCO3 buffer solution (pH=8.8). The inset plot shows the linear region of the curve (n=3).

(A) Fluorescence quenching effect of NSCDs to the addition of different interfering substances and fluazinam under 0.05M Na2CO3-0.1MNaHCO3 (pH=8.8) buffer solution. F0 and F corresponded to the fluorescence intensities of NSCDs in the absence and presence of interfering substance or fluazinam. The concentration of fluazinam is 1μM, while the concentration of each analogues was set 5-fold of fluazinam, the concentration of other ions, methyl parathion and carbaryl was selected as 20-fold of fluazinam. (B)Zeta potential of NSCDs in 0.05M Na2CO3-0·1MNaHCO3 (pH=8.8) buffer solution.

Photographs of the filter paper before (A) and after (B) immersion in NSCDs under irradiation of UV light (312nm). Photographs of the test paper with different amount of fluazinam (from left to right: 20, 10, 2, 1, 0.2, 0.1nM, blank) under irradiation of (C) sunlight and (D) UV light (312nm).

Schematic illustration of the sensitive fluorescence detection of fluazinam based on NSCDs. (represents π-π stacking effect; represents the hydrogen interaction; represents the interaction between the electron-rich groups and electron-deficient groups.).
Determinations of fluazinam in soil and apple samples by the proposed chemo-sensor and HPLC.

head.ce:title: An efficient fluorescent probe for fluazinam using N, S co-doped carbon dots from l-cysteine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Biorheology Science and Technology, Ministry of Education, College of Bioengineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing, 400044, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Biorheology Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical and Testing center, Sichuan university of Science and Engineering, Zigong 643000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sichuan university of Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University, Chongqing 400044, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: National Engineering Research Center of Solid-State Brewing, Luzhou Laojiao Group CO. Ltd., Luzhou, Sichuan, 646000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Engineering Research Center of Solid-State Brewing

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots doped with nitrogen and sulfur (NSCDs) was prepared from l-cysteine with a hydrothermal method and applied to develop a fluorescent sensor to determine fluazinam. Characterizations of the NSCDs suggest that it provides abundant affinitive sites for recognizing fluazinam through molecular interactions including electrostatic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions. Strong inner filter effect (IFE) leads fluazinam to quench the fluorescence of the NSCDs with high efficiency. Under optimized conditions, the fluorescent probe shows very good sensitivity and selectivity in a linear range from 0.05μM to 4μM, and the detection limit is calculated down to 10nM. The proposed fluazinam sensor is demonstrated to be feasible for application in detecting this fungicide in soil and apple samples with satisfactory accuracy. Besides, NSCDs could also be used to fabricate visual paper-based testing stripes under a portable UV lamp with a detection limit as lower as 50μM. The fluorescent material presented here provides a functional platform for simple yet facile in-field detection of fluazinam in related samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluazinam, first introduced by a Japanese company in the 1990s, is a broad-spectrum fungicide based on uncoupling oxidative phosphorylation in fungal pathogens [1]. Due to its fungicidal activity against a wide range of crop diseases, it has been widely applied in many countries and regions [2,3]. However, sub-chronic and chronic toxicity studies suggest that fluazinam is found to target liver, lung, uterus, testes, pancreas, thymus, thyroid, stomach, eyes and brain and can causes dermatitis [4] asthma [5] in human. Furthermore, Lee has proved that fluazinam produces dose-dependent cytotoxicity and decreases the tyrosine hydroxylase expression in SH-SY5Y cell [6,7]. Excessive exposure to fluazinam, along with genetic predispositions, poses a major contributing factor for the development of neurodegenerative diseases, such as Parkinson's disease. Thus, it is urgent to monitor the levels of fluazinam in related products.

Currently, a variety of analytical methods have been reported for fluazinam determination, which include gas chromatography [8], gas chromatography cascade mass spectrum (GC–MS)[9], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10]. Although these methods are demonstrated with very good sensitivity and accuracy, most of them are limited by some serious disadvantages, such as time-consuming, requirement of professional and complicated operation, and also high cost, thus restricting their popularity for general in-field application. In the meantime, fluorescent spectroscopy alternates a powerful optical method to provide a nondestructive, ultrasensitive, and simple detection platform free from complicated pretreated processes. However, the conventional fluorescence methods for detecting non-fluorescent fluazinam need fluorescent probe or derivationation. Because the employment of fluorescent probe is facile than derivationation, it is of great importance to explore novel fluorescent probes that are highly sensitive and selective for the determination of fluazinam. Accordingly, organic dyes, semiconductor quantum dots and carbon nanodots (CDs) have been widely used as fluorescent sensors to detect a variety of ions and biological molecules. While organic dyes involve in disadvantages like poor photo-stability, narrow excitation spectra and broad absorption bands with red tailing [11], semiconductor quantum dots suffer from intrinsic limitations such as expensive synthesis routes, potential toxicity from heavy metals (such as cadmium) and environmental hazards [12,13]. In view of those drawbacks, CDs stands out as a good candidate due to stable photoluminescence [14], excellent biocompatibility [15,16], low cytotoxicity [17,18], good dispersibility in water [19,20], chemical inertness and easy modulation [21–25]. These properties promise CDs to be a useful tool to develop fluorescent chemo-sensors and biosensors for the detection of a variety of analytes [26–37]. The major shortcoming of CDs-based sensing systems is that CDs cannot selectively identify a target with sensitive response [38,39]. Great efforts have been made to modify CDs, in which surface functionalization [40,41] and heteroatom doping are the two major strategies to obtain efficient modification. However, surface modification with polymers or small organic molecules is usually complicated due to unavoidable purification of resultant functionalized CDs. Therefore, doping CDs with heteroatoms [42] such as N [43–45], S [46], P [47],B [48] and F [49], especially N-doping or co-doping with N or other elements [50–52], is recently considered as a more effective approach to change the band gap, avoid self-quenching, tune optical and electrical properties, and also enhance chemical activity for practical applications. For example, N-doping was applied to alter the properties of the quantum dots for application in size-dependent electro-catalytic activity for the oxygen reduction reaction (ORR) and metal-free ORR catalysts in fuel cell [45]. Boron-doping improved the specificity of CDs for selective glucose detection over its close isomeric cousins due to interaction between cis-diol units in glucose and boronic groups [53,54]. And S atom in CDs is important for tuning the electronic local density as well as promoting the coordination interaction between Fe3+ and phenolic hydroxyl groups on the on the edge of sulfur doped CDs, providing a highly selective fluorescent probe for Fe3+ detection. Rather from other carbon dots that are often susceptible to pH without additional surface passivation, the F-doping carbon dots can resist pH changing and display stable luminescence in both acid and alkali conditions.

To the best of our knowledge, limited chemo-sensors for trace fluazinam have been reported up to date, let alone using CDs as fluorescent chemo-sensors. Inspired by the outstanding performance of heteroatom doped CDs, we herein report N, S co-doped carbon dots (NSCDs) using l-cysteine as the C, N and S sources through a facile hydrothermal route. The strong molecular interactions (electrostatic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions) between NSCDs and fluazinam lead to a decrease in the fluorescence intensity (quenching) of NSCDs due to IFE. The NSCDs-based fluorescence assay can be successfully applied to selectively detect fluazinam with high sensitivity and high accuracy. As far as we know, this is the first report realizing successful determination of fluazinam in real samples like soil and fruits using NSCDs as a fluorescent probe.

l-cysteine was purchased from Tianjin Guangfu Chemical Reagents Co. Ltd. (Tianjin, China). Quinine sulfate dehydrate was obtained from Shanghai Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China). HPLC grade acetonitrile was obtained from Fisher Scientific (Belgium). Dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 1000Da) was obtained from Shanghai Green bird Science &Technology Development Co. (Shanghai, China). Fluazinam assay standard and all other chemicals (analytical-reagent grade) acquired from Aladdin Chemistry Reagent Company (Shanghai, China) without further purification. The ultrapure water used in all the experiments was purified by a Milli-Q system (Millipore, 18.2MΩ, France).

Fluorescence spectra were measured with a fluorescence spectrometer PerkinElmer LS55 (PE, US). A TU-1901 double-beam UV–vis spectrophotometer (Peking General Instrument Co. Ltd., Beijing, China) was used to record absorption spectra and measure absorbance. A JEM-2100 microscope (JEOL, Japan) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200kV was applied to characterize the morphology and size of as-prepared carbon dots. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were recorded on a Spectrum GX spectrometer (PE, US) in the form of KBr wafer technique, scanning from 4000 to 400cm−1. Zeta potential was measured using a zetasizer (Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, UK). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analyses were conducted using a Kratos XSAM800 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, UK). The incident radiation came from a monochromatic Al X-ray (1486.6ev) at 12kV and 15mA. The crystal phases of the NSCDs were identified by powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis using a Bruker/D2 PHASER (Bruker, Germany) instrument using Cu Kα radiation (λ=0.15406nm).

NSCDs were prepared using following procedures: 0.1g l-cysteine and 5mL glycerol were added to 10mL ultrapure water. After ultrasonic treatment for 10min, the mixture was bubbling by N2 for 15min to remove dissolved O2 and subsequently put into a 50mL Teflon-lined autoclave to allow for reaction at 180°C for 12h. Resultant brown-yellow solution was naturally cooled down to room temperature, and then centrifuged at 15000 rpms for 15min and the supernatant was collected to dialyze using a membrane with molecular weight cutoff of 1000 for 24h, followed by keeping in the dark for future use.

Fluorescent measurements were conducted at an excitation wavelength of 350nm, with a slit width of 10nm both for excitation and emission. In a typical assay, 200μL of the stock solution of NSCDs was added to 2.0mL 0.05M Na2CO3-0.1M NaHCO3 buffer solution, followed by the addition of different amounts fluazinam or sample solution. Then the mixture was diluted with ultrapure water to 8mL and homogenized thoroughly with a vortex. After incubated for 22min at 80 °C, the solution was transferred for FL measurement. All the fluorescence intensities were repeatedly measured at least three parallel samples to obtain average intensities for further data analysis.

Structural analogues of fluazinam including fluvalinate, 3-fluorine benzylamine and benfluralin, possible interfering compounds like methyl parathion and carbaryl, as well as metal ions (K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Hg2+), anion (SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−), were extensively investigated as coexisting substances. The concentration of each analogues was set as 5-fold of fluazinam, the concentration of K+, Mg2+ , Ca2+, NO3 −, Cl− were set as 200-fold of fluazinam, while the concentration of other ions, methyl parathion and carbaryl was selected as 20-fold of fluazinam. The same detection conditions mentioned above were applied for the investigation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of fluazinam in soil and apple samples
ce:section.ce:para: 50mL acetonitrile was added in a 5g soil or a 20g apple sample and the mixture was homogenized by a high-speed homogenizer for 2min and then filtered in a 100mL stoppered graduated cylinder. 6g sodium chloride was added in the filtrate and the mixture obtained was shaken and extracted for 2min. After standing to the stratification the organic and aqueous phases were separated, and 10mL organic phase was put on water bath under 60°C blew to dryness. The residue was dissolved in 5mL methanol and filtered through 0.22μm filter for detection.

The free fluorescein filter paper was soaked in NSCDs stock solution overnight. After the solvent was evaporated thoroughly, different quantities of fluazinam were dripped on the filter paper. The test paper was then allowed to evaporate the solvent sufficient at room temperature. The digital image of the test paper was obtained under a portable 312nm UV lamp.

Fig. 4 shows the remarkable optical properties of as-obtained NSCDs confirmed by UV–vis absorption and fluorescent (FL) spectra on its aqueous solution. The UV–vis spectrum exhibits two characteristic absorption peaks at around 209nm and 347nm. The absorption peak centered at 209nm with no clear FL signal is attributed to the π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and indicates the existence of aromatic heterocycle. The other absorption peak at 347nm with strong emission is resulted from the trapping of excited-state energy by the surface states. Very bright blue fluorescence can be clearly seen in low NSCDs concentration (40μg/mL) under the illumination of UV light (312nm) in the inset of Fig. 4 and confirms that the NSCDs has a strong blue fluorescence due to non-emissive light can be observed from the solution of L-cysteine at 312nm. The emission spectrum of NSCDs (Fig. 4B) indicates that the emission wavelength of NSCDs is excitation-dependent. When the excitation wavelength changes from 300nm to 400nm, the maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength are observed individually at 350nm and 440nm with a Stokes shift of 90nm. Excitation-dependent FL behaviors of NSCDs reflect effect from particles of different sizes and a distribution of different surface states [50], which is in agreement with the TEM characterization. The relative PL QY of NSCDs is as high as 15.2% in reference to Quinine sulfate (QY=0.54 at 360nm) dissolved in H2SO4 (0.1M, refractive index 1.33), indicating strong quantum confinement of nanoparticles which is beneficial as a highly sensitive material for chemo-sensor.

No perceptible change of fluorescence intensity is observed at 440nm under ultraviolet light for 3h. Moreover, storage of aqueous NSCDs (3.5mg/mL) for six months at a room temperature did not resulted in obvious aggregation, demonstrating the good stability of the NSCDs in solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of the NSCDs probe for fluazinam detection
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve sensitive measurement of fluazinam, the impact of pH, buffer medium, volume of buffer solution, temperature and reaction time on the fluorescence quenching of NSCDs were optimized. As shown in Fig. 5 A, the fluorescence quenching of NSCDs changes as a result of varied pH of the solution. When the pH of the solution increases from 2 to 7, the fluorescence quenching of NSCDs is improved and then flats with pH between 7 and 10. As the pH exceeds 10, the fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs begins to decrease whenever fluazinam presence or not. F0/F increases slightly at the pH is 11.4 because the fluorescence intensity decrease more sever when fluazinam is presence. However, data of fluorescence intensities become poor reproducible and the linear range is narrowed when pH is set at 11.4. As a result, the optimal pH value is set at 8.8 which lies the middle of the platform. Since the fluorescence quenching is observed maximized in 0.05M Na2CO3-0.1M NaHCO3 buffer solution among several buffer mediums at pH 8.8 (Fig. 5B), it is chosen as preferable buffer medium. Likely, the optimized volume of buffer medium, temperature and reaction time are set at 2mL, 80°C and 22min, respectively (Fig. 5C–F).

Under the optimized conditions, different concentrations of fluazinam ranging from 0 to 6μM were mixed with NSCDs to evaluate the analytical performance of the probe. The result shown in Fig. 6 indicates that fluorescence quenching of NSCDs toward fluazinam locates in a concentration range from 0.05 to 4μM, which can be fitted into Stern-Volmer type equation F0/F=0.9556+0.5421C with a correlation coefficient R2 of 0.9957. C was the concentration of fluazinam, F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensity of the NSCDs in the presence and absence of fluazinam. The limit of detection (LOD) is determined to be 10nM calculated as signal/noise ratio of 3. According to the regulation of European Food Safety Authority, the maximal acceptable concentrations of fluazinam are 0.65μM and 0.11μM in pome fruit, Berries & small fruit and Citrus fruit, respectively. So that the fluorescent probe presented here is sensitive enough to monitor fluazinam amount in fruit samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the NSCDs probe for fluazinam detection
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of NSCDs for the measurement of fluazinam, the fluorescence intensities of NSCDs were analyzed in the presence of a group of possible interfering counterparts including K+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Ag+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, Hg2+, SO4 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, methyl parathion, carbaryl, fluvalinate, 3-fluorobenzylamine and benfluralin. Results in Fig. 7 A show that no obvious decrease of fluorescence intensity is observed after the addition of all those metal ions, anions and methyl parathion, carbaryl, fluvalinate, 3-fluorobenzylamine. Despite that benfluralin induces slight interference with 5-times higher concentration than fluazinam, it is not unreasonable to claim the good selectivity of the probe. The outstanding selectivity of NSCDs toward fluazinam may be attribute to the fact that fluazinam has more electron-deficient groups (such as CF3, −NO2) than other compounds, thus more strong molecular interactions (electrostatic, π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions). So that a stronger affinity of fluazinam toward the electron-rich surface of NSCDs is realized instead of those possible coexisting substances. Moreover, NSCDs shows a negative zeta potential of −31.6mV in 0.05M Na2CO3-0.1M NaHCO3 (pH=8.8) buffer solution (Fig. 7B). Thus, the strong electrostatic repulsion effect would prevent anions interacted with NSCDs due to charge interaction and NSCDs can resist the interferences of anion. Although cations might interact with NSCDs through electro-static affinity, they are not able to produce measurable interference, may be ascribed to three reasons weaken the interference. The first is the pH of the system is 8.8 and under such low pH, many cations may be hydrolyzed, such as Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+,Hg2+, Pb2+, Ag+. The second is there is a lot of CO3 2− in the measure system which may interact with many cations, such as Ca2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+. The third is most of the hydrolysis products or the carbonates and Na+, K+ can't absorb the excitation or emission light of NSCDs which lead there is no IFE between cations and NSCDs.

Since introduction of sulfur atoms would enhance the effect of nitrogen atoms on the properties of the doped carbon nanomaterials through a cooperative effect [46], we envisioned that the NSCDs can interact with fluazinam through three kinds of interactions: 1) Strong electrostatic interaction between the electron-rich −NH2,-OH, −COO− at the surface of NSCDs and electron-deficient nitro-aromatic rings and fluoro-substituted aromatic ring of fluazinam. 2) Hydrogen bonding between OH, −NH2, −COO− at the surface of the NSCDs and fluazinam. 3) π-π stacking between aromatic heterocyclic of NSCDs and pyridyl and phenyl of fluazinam. Those specific interactions together make NSCDs target fluazinam with high selectivity for the determination of fluazinam based on the inner filter effect (IFE).

It is required to obtain high efficiency for the IFE that there is a good spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber and the excitation band and/or emission band of the fluorophore. Therefore, it is important to choose a suitable absorber to couple the fluorophore pair for the IFE-based fluorescent chemo-sensor. As shown in Fig. 4C, the UV–vis spectrum of fluazinam in 0.05M Na2CO3-0.1M NaHCO3 (pH=8.8) buffer solution exhibited a characteristic absorption peak at 347nm, overlapped the excitation band of the NSCDs, and thus absorb the emission light from NSCDs. Therefore, the absorbance enhancement of fluazinam could be successfully converted to fluorescence quenching of NSCDs, which made the IFE occurs in a highly efficient way. The possible mechanism is shown in Scheme 1 .

The applicability of the developed method was tested using fluazinam (0.5, 1, 1.5μM) spiked soil and apple samples. The obtained recoveries vary from 96.0% to 102.0% (See Table 1 ). The relative standard deviation (RSD) of each sample is below 4.7%, revealing the high reproducibility and precision. Furthermore, we used HPLC [10] to detect fluazinam in spiked apple samples and soil samples for comparison to further investigate the performance of the proposed fluorescent detection method. The obtained results are shown in Table 1 and Supplementary materials. T test analysis (confidence level 95%) suggests no significant difference between these two methods. Due to apparent advantages involving high sensitivity, simple operation, low-cost, time-saving and convenient analysis, this proposed chemo-sensor would offer a good alternative for rapid detection of fluazinam in related environmental and fruits samples.

We finally investigate the feasibility to fabricate NSCDs-based test paper for in-field fluazinam detection by immersion filter paper free of fluorescein into the stock solution of NSCDs overnight. The structure of the NSCDs allows them to be stacked together in the pores of the cellulose matrices without self-quenching and gives the test paper with strong blue fluorescence under UV light (Fig. 8 B). The color of the zones in the test paper with the addition of high concentration of fluazinam changed to light yellow under sunlight (Fig. 8C), but clear quenching of the bright blue fluorescence of the test paper can be observed under a 312nm UV lamp shown in Fig. 8D. The detection limits of such test paper are measured to be 2.5mM and 50μM under sunlight and UV light, respectively. Compared with other test paper reported, this presented here is more advantageous, since it is economical, biocompatible and nontoxic. Therefore, it might alternate a promising visual detection strip for facile in-field surveillance of fluazinam contamination in related products.

Reliable and facile in-field detection of pesticide and fungicide residues like fluazinam in related samples remains a challenging task for food surveillance and quality control, which is urgently required to minimize possible toxic threat of those compounds. Using a simple hydrothermal method, nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots is prepared with a quantum yield up to 15.2%. Abundant functional groups in the NSCDs surface lead to higher affinity toward fluazinam due to interactions including electrostatic interaction, hydrogen bonding and also π-π stacking. As a result of high-efficient IFE, a fluorescent probe based on as-prepared NSCDs is fabricated so as to determine trace amount of fluazinam ranging from 0.05 to 4μM. The lowest detection limit is realized at 10nM with very remarkable selectivity and accuracy, demonstrated upon successful analysis of spiked soil and fruit samples in good reproducibility. Those findings suggest that the proposed fluorescent probe can serve as high-performance sensor for the monitoring of trace fluazinam in environment and fruit through not only fluorescent intensity but also paper-based visual measurements. Besides, the present study also presents a useful strategy to fabricate simple, cost-effective, eco-friendly, and also efficient fluorescent sensors for related compounds.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by a grant from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31171684), Key Technologies R&D Program of China (No. 2014BAD07B02), China Postdoctoral Science Foundation Funded Project (2015M582522), the Academician workstation in Sichuan Province GY2015-01, Chongqing Graduate Student Research Innovation Project (No. CYB14013), Foundation of Sichuan Educational Committee (12ZA265) and sharing fund of Chongqing University's large equipment.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.07.169.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Uncoupling activity of a newly developed fungicide, fluazinam 3-chloro-N-(3-chloro-2,6-dinitro-4-trifluoromethylphenyl)-5-trifluorome thyl-2-pyridinamine

Allergic contact-dermatitis from the newly introduced fungicide fluazinam
Occupational asthma from fungicides fluazinam and chlorothalonil

Fluazinam targets mitochondrial complex I to induce reactive oxygen species-dependent cytotoxicity in SH-SY5Y cells
Fate of azoxystrobin, fluazinam, kresoxim-methyl, mepanipyrim, and tetraconazole from vine to wine
Gas chromatographic determination of azoxystrobin, fluazinam, kresoxim-methyl, mepanipyrim, and tetraconazole in grapes must, and wine
Ecological impact in ditch mesocosms of simulated spray drift from a crop protection program for potatoes
In situ building of a nanoprobe based on fluorescent carbon dots for methylmercury detection
Bandgap-like strong fluorescence in functionalized carbon nanoparticles
Computational and ultrastructural toxicology of a nanoparticle, quantum dot 705, in mice
Red, green, and blue luminescence by carbon dots: full-color emission tuning and multicolor cellular imaging
Bioimaging of hyaluronic acid derivatives using nanosized carbon dots
Highly biocompatible carbon nanodots for simultaneous bioimaging and targeted photodynamic therapy in vitro and In vivo
One pot synthesis of highly luminescent polyethylene glycol anchored carbon dots functionalized with a nuclear localization signal peptide for cell nucleus imaging
Integrating oxaliplatin with highly luminescent carbon dots: an unprecedented theranostic agent for personalized medicine
Preparation of functionalized water-soluble photoluminescent carbon quantum dots from petroleum coke
Water-soluble fluorescent carbon quantum dots and photocatalyst design
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots for chemical sensing
A hydrothermal route to water-stable luminescent carbon dots as nanosensors for pH and temperature
Amino-functionalized green fluorescent carbon dots as surface energy transfer biosensors for hyaluronidase
Surface chemistry routes to modulate the photoluminescence of graphene quantum dots: from fluorescence mechanism to up-conversion bioimaging applications
Dopamine fluorescent sensors based on polypyrrole/graphene quantum dots core/shell hybrids
Facile synthesis of N, S-codoped fluorescent carbon nanodots for fluorescent resonance energy transfer recognition of methotrexate with high sensitivity and selectivity
Carbon quantum dots-Based recyclable real-time fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase with adenosine triphosphate as substrate

Carbon-Dot-Based dual-Emission nanohybrid produces a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for in vivo imaging of cellular copper ions
Facile synthesis of graphene quantum dots from 3D graphene and their application for Fe3+sensing
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
Carbon-dot-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for imaging and biosensing of superoxide anion in live cells
Nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon dots for highly selective and sensitive detection of Hg (II) ions
Highly sensitive and selective fluorescent detection of cerebral lead(II) based on graphene quantum dot conjugates
Strongly green-photoluminescent graphene quantum dots for bioimaging applications
Creating high yield water soluble luminescent graphene quantum dots via exfoliating and disintegrating carbon nanotubes and graphite flakes
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Non-metal single/dual doped carbon quantum dots: a general flame synthetic method and electro-catalytic properties
Synthesis and unique photoluminescence properties of nitrogen-rich quantum dots and their applications
Amplified spontaneous green emission and lasing emission from carbon nanoparticles
Nitrogen-Doped graphene quantum dots with oxygen-rich functional groups
Sulfur-doped graphene quantum dots as a novel fluorescent probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of Fe3+
Low temperature synthesis of highly stable phosphate functionalized two color carbon nanodots and their application in cell imaging
Green and simple route toward boron doped carbon dots with significantly enhanced non-linear optical properties
To lose is to gain: effective synthesis of water-soluble graphene fluoroxide quantum dots by sacrificing certain fluorine atoms from exfoliated fluorinated graphene
Carbon-Based dots Co-doped with nitrogen and sulfur for high quantum yield and excitation-independent emission
Highly luminescent S, N co-doped graphene quantum dots with broad visible absorption bands for visible light photocatalysts
Hair fiber as a precursor for synthesizing of sulfur- and nitrogen-co-doped carbon dots with tunable luminescence properties
Synthesis-Modification integration one-Step fabrication of boronic acid functionalized carbon dots for fluorescent blood sugar sensing
Boron-doped graphene quantum dots for selective glucose sensing based on the abnormal aggregation-Induced photoluminescence enhancement
Broad family of carbon nanoallotropes: classification, chemistry, and applications of fullerenes carbon dots, nanotubes, graphene, nanodiamonds, and combined superstructures
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots for multicolor patterning, sensors, and bioimaging

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Shiying Zou received MS degree in Polymeric Chemistry and Physics at Sichuan University, People's Republic of China. She is presently a Ph. D. student in College of Bioengineering at Chongqing University, People's Republic of China. Her research interests include the development of new nanomaterials for application in optoelectronic devices and drug analysis.

Changjun Hou is presently a full professor in College of Bioengineering at Chongqing University, People's Republic of China. He received his Ph. D. degree from Chongqing University, China, in 2004. His scientific interests are the design and construction of biochemical sensing systems based on porphyrins and other sensitive materials.

Huanbao Fa is presently an associated professor in College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Chongqing University. He received his Ph. D. degree from Jilin University, China, in 2006. His research interest focuses on the development of novel nanomaterials and their applications in Electrochemistry.
Liang Zhang is a senior engineer of Luzhou Laojiao Group CO. Ltd. His research interest focuses on the food safety and quality control.

Yi Ma is presently a Ph. D. student in College of Bioengineering at Chongqing University, People's Republic of China. His research interest focuses on the development of novel nanomaterials and their applications in natural products.

Liang Dong is presently a Ph. D. student in College of Bioengineering at Chongqing University, People's Republic of China. His research interest focuses on the development of novel nanomaterials and their applications.

Dan Li received her BS degree from Huazhong Agricultural University, People's Republic of China, in 2013. Her research interest focuses on the development of novel nanomaterials and their applications in natural products.

Danqun Huo is a professor in College of Bioengineering, Chongqing University, China. Her current research interests include the development of chemical sensors and sensor-array, Electrochemistry and analytical microsystems.
